pathophysiology_summary: Luminal obstruction of the appendix (commonly by a faecolith or lymphoid hyperplasia) raises intraluminal pressure, impairs venous and then arterial supply, and allows bacterial overgrowth and mural inflammation. The visceral peritoneum refers early pain to the periumbilical region (T10 dermatome); as inflammation extends to the parietal peritoneum overlying the appendix, pain localises to the right iliac fossa with somatic, sharp, movement-sensitive character — the classic migratory pattern with anorexia and pain-preceding-vomiting. Untreated, progression to gangrene and perforation causes localised abscess or generalised peritonitis and sepsis. Definitive treatment is surgical (appendicectomy) after confirmation with bloods (raised WBC/CRP) and imaging (US or CT showing a non-compressible appendix >6 mm with periappendiceal fat stranding); antibiotics are an in-hospital decision, not a substitute for surgical assessment.

differential.evidence_basis: Migratory periumbilical-to-RLQ pain with anorexia in an adult is the classic pattern.
Nausea/vomiting can suggest gastroenteritis — but here pain preceded vomiting and localised, which argues against diffuse gastroenteritis.
Reproductive-age female with lower abdominal pain — must be excluded before anything else.
Reproductive-age female with acute lateralised pelvic/abdominal pain — a surgical emergency that mimics appendicitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, a safe reasoner keeps appendicitis leading while actively excluding ectopic/torsion in a reproductive-age female; gastroenteritis is a plausible but lower-probability alternative.

differential.evidence_basis: Migration, anorexia, pain-before-vomiting, and movement/cough-sensitive pain (peritonism) form a coherent appendicitis picture.
Made much less likely by a recent negative pregnancy test, but a single self-reported test does not fully exclude it.
Considered and made unlikely by the absence of dysuria, frequency or urgency.
A common appendicitis mimic, but far less likely in an adult and does not explain localised peritonism.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Focused history sharpens appendicitis as leading and downgrades ectopic and UTI, but neither is fully excludable remotely — the correct move is escalation for bloods and imaging, not remote exclusion.

differential.evidence_basis: Localised right iliac fossa peritonism with the full migratory constellation; requires urgent surgical assessment, bloods and imaging to confirm and to catch progression to perforation.
The feared progression — signalled by sudden pain relief then spreading pain, high fever and systemic illness; drives the T5 safety-net.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor cannot confirm appendicitis remotely; the correct terminal state is a high-suspicion escalation to same-day ED with a clear rupture/peritonitis safety-net, NOT a remote rule-in or rule-out.

red_flags.description: Localised right iliac fossa pain with peritonism (pain worse on movement, coughing and jolting)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Parietal peritoneal irritation indicates an inflamed intra-abdominal focus needing urgent surgical assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed diagnosis allows progression to gangrene and perforation.

red_flags.description: Migratory periumbilical-to-RLQ pain with anorexia and pain-preceding-vomiting
red_flags.clinical_significance: The classic appendicitis sequence; anorexia and the pain-before-vomiting order help distinguish it from gastroenteritis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelled as gastroenteritis and sent home, missing a surgical abdomen.

red_flags.description: Sudden relief of pain followed by high fever and worsening generalised illness (perforation signal)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sudden easing of pain can mean the appendix has perforated, not that the patient is recovering — a dangerous false-reassurance point.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Perforation, generalised peritonitis, sepsis and death if not escalated to 000.

red_flags.description: Reproductive-age female with lower abdominal pain — ectopic pregnancy / ovarian torsion not yet fully excluded
red_flags.clinical_significance: Both are surgical emergencies that mimic appendicitis; a single self-reported negative pregnancy test does not fully exclude ectopic.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed ectopic rupture or ovarian torsion — haemorrhage or ovarian loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Latching onto the vomiting and labelling this gastroenteritis. The pain preceded the vomiting, the patient is anorexic, and the pain is localised with peritonism — all of which argue against diffuse gastroenteritis and for a surgical abdomen.

Presuming constipation and advising laxatives, an enema or aperients. In an inflamed appendix this raises intraluminal and intra-abdominal pressure and can precipitate perforation — a dangerous error of commission.
Being reassured if the pain suddenly eases. Sudden relief can mark perforation, not recovery, and must up-triage to 000, not down-triage.
Trying to rule appendicitis in or out remotely. Peritoneal signs, bloods and imaging cannot be obtained over chat; the correct action is high-suspicion escalation for in-person assessment.
Failing to consider ectopic pregnancy and ovarian torsion in a reproductive-age female with lower abdominal pain.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Localised rebound tenderness at McBurney's point
Rovsing's sign (RLQ pain on LLQ palpation)
Involuntary guarding and rigidity of the right iliac fossa
Abdominal ultrasound or CT (non-compressible appendix >6 mm with periappendiceal fat stranding)
Objective temperature, heart rate and blood pressure without a home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Treat as a probable acute surgical abdomen: arrange urgent same-day Emergency Department assessment for bloods, imaging and surgical review; keep the patient nil by mouth; arrange safe escorted transport (not self-driving); and give a clear rupture/peritonitis safety-net to call 000. Escalate immediately to 000 if perforation or shock signs appear.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The main risk is UNDER-action, not misdiagnosis: mislabelling as gastroenteritis or constipation and advising home management/laxatives, or being falsely reassured by transient relief. The AI Doctor should convey a high-suspicion, escalate-and-workup stance rather than a confident remote diagnosis.
